You can try to fix it in Pint but I think that the calculation will be efficient enough for every day use (I might be wrong here). Additionally, it will be annoying to implement. Pint calculates the conversion factor and then multiplies it by the magnitude. The conversion factor is calculated as float by multiplying floats (which are obtained from parsing the definitions). So yo will need to change the parser works to output fractions.

There is simpler, fast (both to run and implement), not so accurate alternative which might be good enough for you. In the UnitRegistry code locate the function that does the conversion and change the conversion factor to Fraction using the `Fraction.from_float` when the magnitude is a fraction. The relevant code starts here:

https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/master/pint/unit.py#L1109

If it performs well, I will be happy to merge i.

This looks like a valid issue:
```
   The value in the length field MUST contain the length of the Value
   part of the attribute, prior to padding, measured in bytes.  Since
   STUN aligns attributes on 32-bit boundaries, attributes whose content
   is not a multiple of 4 bytes are padded with 1, 2, or 3 bytes of
   padding so that its value contains a multiple of 4 bytes.  The
   padding bits are ignored, and may be any value.
```

> The heart of the matter is the separation of rules for the project and machine-specific rules, detailing things such as which compilers to use (mpifort, mpiifort, whatever these are named), compiler flags and locations of libraries

You have to be patient with, my English is very poor. My understanding was that `fobos` contains the **project rules** and `fobos.inc` contains the **machine specific ones**, is it right?

> Build rules and general configurations should be under git, but machine-specific configurations (and debug options etc) not (in my opinion). For this some templates can be provided, but it is quite inconvenient to have a 'dirty' makefile in your directory, both with regard to accidentally commiting and with changing things in the project-wide makefile.

> To make this your approach would be sufficient, but different section names are unnecessary.

Oh, mine was an overkill... you just want **only 1 container-section** (whatever named), is it right? 

> I think it would be best to only give a warning if a key does not exist, in that way libraries can be disabled without changing the main fobos file.

Yes, this is could be viable. 

To summarize my new understanding you want something like:

#### fobos.inc

```ini
[section-of-any-name]
lapack = /opt/arch/lapack3.1.1
```

#### fobos

```ini
[default]
compiler = gnu
libs = $fobos.inc(lapack) $fobos.inc(fft)
```

that will warn for missing `fft` key without stopping (or reverting back to some default value for the library).

What do you think?

Hello!

Good question! Out of curiosity, what kind of thing are you fitting that you don't know the number of *dependent* variables? How can you know what data it corresponds with? For feeding your data to `Fit` you can use `**` dict unpacking:

```python3
x = variables('...')
a, b = parameters('...')
dependent_vars = []
for idx in range(num_dep_vars):
    var = Variable(name='var_{}')
    dependent_vars.append(var)

model = {dv: a*x+b for dv in dependent_vars}
data = {dv.name: data[idx] for (idx, dv) in enumerate(dependent_vars)}
fit = Fit(model, **data)
result = fit.execute()
```

Hi,

That is difficult to tell. There is always some tradeoff. I would maybe rerun the scaffolding with -e 10 or something to remove creating links between contigs that has low support. Because you seem to have a high coverage. The parameter -e is actually inferred by besst automatically in latest develop version --- but I haven't  released it to master yet.. But I think 10 could be suitable for you data set. -e is set to 3 by default by the way.

Now, if you really want to optimize, I would run several runs with BESST for different -e and take the one that gives relatively best tradeoff between contiguity (highest N50 and lowest number of scaffolds) with the highest value of -e -- If there exist such a "clear" winner. That is, my prediction is that lower -e will scaffold more, but might also be more error prone. If you would try e.g. -e from 3 to 20 (with some stepsize) and pick the highest value of -e that is "relatively good performing" (whatever that means) compared to lower values on -e.

Best,
K

Hi @1ntell1gentDB,

I looked into this in more detail. The algorithms to calculate LoS, diffraction, troposcatter, and ducting are quite similar in P.452-16 and P.1812-4, but not exactly equal. For example, the latter completely neglects the atmospheric loss terms - which of course don't play a big role at the VHF/UHF frequencies. The biggest difference is in clutter handling. P.452 assumes no clutter in the complete height profile and applies end-point correction. P.1812 adds clutter heights to terrain heights for each position on the propagation path. 

Unfortunately, some heavy API changes seem necessary, if one wanted to have several methods offered to the user within the pathprof sub-package. I think, I might do this at some point, but it will certainly take time. A quicker solution would probably be, to use the P.452 algorithm and apply some pre- and post-processing. This will not give the exact same results as P.1812 (at the moment there is no way to leave out the LoS focussing/multipath correction and atmospheric attenuation in pycraf's calculations), but it will be **very close**. I'm willing to help you implementing the clutter/building entry loss functions of P.1812 as well as the location-percent distribution.

Do you have some example, which we could use for that? I have especially in mind, what database you use for clutter. We'd also need to find a solution for the problem, that SRTM data are in part influenced by clutter (e.g., a forest usually increases the terrain height in SRTM).

You probably have uninitialized variables in your code.  You are also using global variables and raw pointers.  So that kind of error is not at all surprising.

After thinking about this over the weekend, I've got say this behavior really surprises me.  Your mantra throughout seems to be that surprise is bad, and that you should make it easy to use the library correctly and hard to use it incorrectly.  I agree with you 100% there.

Throwing an exception once -- but not twice -- is definitely a significant departure from the norm and therefore surprising.  Throwing an exception that is dependent on compilation flags is another significant departure from the norm.  When that conditional exception is thrown from code that is generally *not* used as a normal shared library but instead compiled inline as part of my library or executable and is further influenced by the manner in which *my* code is compiled, the surprise factor jumps up again.

I get your desire to lessen your support by catering to users "who have basically no idea what they are doing".  I'd much rather you be spending your time making DLIB even more awesome than having to debug other people's code for them.  But I think the cost here is a significant increase in the surprise factor, and making it easy to use incorrectly.

Anticipating your response, I think you would say a segfault-on-second-exception makes it harder to use incorrectly, because in your mind an exception means you are using it incorrectly.  If that is the case, why wait for the second exception?  It's inconsistent.  Either the error is truly fatal (and you continue to believe DLIB is the most important thing a user could being doing with the process) and the process should exit immediately.  Or, the error is not truly fatal and was detected by logic and is therefore recoverable.

Your library is not the operating system.  It's neither your responsibility -- nor should it be in your purview -- to close the user's application for doing something "wrong".  Further, doing so conditionally based on compiler flags is *highly* irregular.  Consider std::vector for expected, normal behavior.  Users may use ::at() if they want bounds checked access, or ::operator[]() if they are willing to accept the consequences and want the speed.  Nowhere is there macro induced behavioral changes.  Nowhere is their forced shutdown if you use ::operator[] out-of-bounds *twice*.  I'm not saying DLIB needs to duplicate API, I'm saying DLIB needs to decide which one of these it's providing, and under no condition does it call ::asset(false) or ::abort() after the *second* exception.

Hey Sriram, sorry it took so long to get back on this. My basic stance on primitives of this level of complexity is that without demonstrating an equivalent level of production-hardenedness, I would find it very difficult to accept such an implementation into boltons. Especially given the following paragraph in [the architecture statement](http://boltons.readthedocs.org/en/latest/architecture.html#design-of-a-bolton): 

> Finally, boltons should be substantial implementations of commonly trivialized stumbling blocks and not the other way around. The larger the problem solved, the less likely the functionality is suitable for inclusion in boltons; boltons are fundamental and self-contained, not sweeping and architecture-defining.

Unfortunately I just don't have the resources or projects right now to take this threadpools out for the requisite spins and rest assured that I've not merged a trivialized version of something I know to be a substantial part of programming.

Finally, Python has a built-in threadpool, in multiprocessing.dummy that can be made to work in a pinch. I've actually used it, and it can certainly be better, but that'll have to wait for another time. Sorry again and thank you for taking the time and creating the contribution all the same.

def as_hdf(self, filepath, mode='w', group='/'):
        """
        Converts the TDMS file into an HDF5 file

        :param filepath: The path of the HDF5 file you want to write to.
        :param mode: The write mode of the HDF5 file. This can be w, a ...
        :param group: A group in the HDF5 file that will contain the TDMS data.
        """
        import h5py

        # Groups in TDMS are mapped to the first level of the HDF5 hierarchy

        # Channels in TDMS are then mapped to the second level of the HDF5
        # hierarchy, under the appropriate groups.

        # Properties in TDMS are mapped to attributes in HDF5.
        # These all exist under the appropriate, channel group etc.

        h5file = h5py.File(filepath, mode)

        container_group = None
        if group in h5file:
            container_group = h5file[group]
        else:
            container_group = h5file.create_group(group)

        # First write the properties at the root level
        try:
            root = self.object()
            for property_name, property_value in root.properties.items():
                container_group.attrs[property_name] = property_value
        except KeyError:
            # No root object present
            pass

        # Now iterate through groups and channels,
        # writing the properties and data
        for group_name in self.groups():
            try:
                group = self.object(group_name)

                # Write the group's properties
                for prop_name, prop_value in group.properties.items():
                    container_group[group_name].attrs[prop_name] = prop_value

            except KeyError:
                # No group object present
                pass

            # Write properties and data for each channel
            for channel in self.group_channels(group_name):
                for prop_name, prop_value in channel.properties.items():
                    container_group.attrs[prop_name] = prop_value

                # This is a workaround for utf-8 types in h5py
                channel_data = None
                if channel.data_type is types.String:
                    channel_data = np.string_(channel.data)
                else:
                    channel_data = channel.data

                container_group[group_name+'/'+channel.channel] = channel.data

        return h5file

How long does your `process_message` take in the worst case? Are you using the default heartbeat and session timeout parameters (it appears so from the consumer side, but you might verify that you haven't modified default server-side configs).

pykafka maintains a custom leader election / partition assignment system, and I don't know the details well enough to comment on it. kafka-python attempts to implement exactly the same group coordination system, algorithms, and configuration parameters as the official java client. But you have found one of the issues with the official implementation, namely that "long" message processing can cause unwanted group rebalance operations and interfere with offset commits etc. Rather than implement and maintain our own system here, I prefer to follow the official implementation. They are currently discussing / implementing a background heartbeat mechanism that should help address. Until then, the recommendation is to tune your heartbeat and session timeouts relative to your worst case message processing time.

The Artemis frame contains two Stomp protocol headers I wouldn't expect. First, it loops through the `receipt` header. This shouldn't be a problem, though. Second, the `subscription` header is added although no `id` header was given (which is optional in Stomp 1.0). The [protocol specification](https://stomp.github.io/stomp-specification-1.0.html) says:

> You can also specify an id header which can then later on be used to UNSUBSCRIBE from the specific subscription as you may end up with overlapping subscriptions using selectors with the same destination. If an id header is supplied then Stomp brokers should append a subscription header to any MESSAGE commands which are sent to the client so that the client knows which subscription the message relates to.

To sum up, you can/should do either of the following:

1. File a bug to Artemis because their implementation of STOMP 1.0 isn't correct and wait until it's fixed.
2. Add an explicit `id`(properly coded, that would be `StompSpec.ID_HEADER`) header to work around the Artemis bug.

Probably you shouldn't use Stomp 1.0 in the first place because it's an under-specified protocol (no `NACK` frames, no header escaping, no heartbeating). Actually, I recommend to start new projects on Stomp 1.2 or (if your broker doesn't support it) Stomp 1.1.

Note that you can open a template (starting-point) presentation of your choice when opening a presentation using `prs = Presentation('arbitrary-deck.pptx')` and that all the masters and layouts from that deck will then be available for your use in the presentation you make from that starting deck (which will often have zero slides, just for that reason).

If that approach doesn't suit, then if you can describe your use case that would help identifying the required features. Slide layouts have a particular set of behaviors, particularly involving adding placeholders, which is not allowed on slides. So while adding a new layout would be a start, there would be a lot more to it.

The diagnostic here is that datas have very different trading lengths (from a practical point of view: *different trading calendars*) and are therefore not time aligned. *backtrader* walks always forward and cannot know that in advance. In addition, the 1st data into the system acts as a *master clock* to keep the buffers synchronized and the other datas may not overtake that *master clock*

Many of the design decisions in the making of *backtrader* have proven to be very flexible. This has proven at least confusing.

This was already discussed in ticket #76 (with `^GSPC` and `^GDAXI` as the assets), in issue #39 and #80 and #115 (this for live data to avoid this effect by increasing the minimum bar period to ensure liquidity synchronizes the assets)

A revision/rewrite of the internals would be needed at this point in time to make this effect disappear.

I don't think this is the correct way to handle escaping of html tags inside the table.

If I understand correctly, the `convert` flag would say "escape all of the contents of all of the `<td>`s" when `True` and "escape none of the contents of any of the `<td>`s" when False. But I don't see a use case for this. You never want to allow everything through unescaped, nor do you want to escape the contents of every `<td>` without thinking.

But you might want to have some html inside the `<td>` of one of your columns. For example: `LinkCol`, which outputs an `<a>`, or `ButtonCol` which outputs a `<button>`.

The way the code currently handles escaping is to allow columns control over the output. They can either specify exactly what goes into the `<td>` (by overriding `td_contents`, like `LinkCol` does) but then the column must remember to do the escaping. OR the column can just reformat the data for display (by overriding `td_format` like `DateCol` does), but then it can't output any html - this is basically a convenience to make it harder to forget the escaping.

The documentation around this may want extending, but it is important that the html escaping is controlled per-column, rather than being done for the whole table.

Thanks for the example code. That always helps.

I don't think this is an XlsxWriter issue. If you removed the XlsxWriter code from your example you would have the same "leak".

If the user creates BytesIO objects then it is up to them to close/clean them up.

It isn't stated or implied in the XlsxWriter documentation that the module will close the BytesIO stream. If fact the [example that demonstrates this usage](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.io/example_images_bytesio.html#ex-images-bytesio) explicitly closes the stream.

In addition, closing the stream may not be what the end user wants.

So overall I think that this issue should be handle in the application code by closing/freeing the stream when it is no longer required.

I've run into similar issues. Any two overlapping merge ranges will cause a similar message in any version of excel.

XLSXWriter already has some things in place to warn about single-cell merge ranges, although I suggest the usual cause is a "merge-if-necessary" request which requires some additional code to preempt the test you're already running. (In other words, rather than warning, please just delegate to write_cell. Or maybe allow a create-time pragma to turn off the warning?)

It's not too complicated to write **_a_** test for overlapping merges (which should ideally be considered an exception at the point of creation, not when the worksheet is finally written out) but it may be challenging to make such a test suitably efficient in the case of many merge ranges. (The naive approach takes O(n^2) time in the number of merge ranges.) The first idea that comes to mind is to store the merge ranges in the form of a space-partitioning tree, but then there's a need to keep that balanced over time, which remains a challenge. I'm sure there is a good documented algorithm for the purpose. Check http://nist.gov/dads and you'll find something good.

ChatterBot currently supports two different dialogue algorithms.

The first is the _closest match_ algorithm that determines the similarity between the statement provided by the user and a set of known statements. For example, based on the sample input you provided, there is a 65% similarity between `"where is the post office?"` and `"looking for the post office"`. The closest match algorithm selects the highest matching known statements and returns a response based on that selection.

The second is the _closest meaning_ algorithm. This algorithm uses the wordnet functionality of [nltk](http://www.nltk.org) to determine the similarity of two statements based on the path similarity between each token in each statement. The sum of the path similarities is used so that the statement that has the closest path similarity (basically the closeness of synonyms) is returned.

* In the case of both algorithms and the training examples you provided, the input of "looking for the post office" would match close enough to "where is the post office?" to return "it is right behind you".

***

So, to answer your questions about what functionality is available:

- **enriching or at least retaining a context during a dialogue**  
(Currently not present.) ChatterBot **does** retain a copy of the current conversation and it a parameter that is provided to all logic adapters. However, it is currently not used. I have plans to create a new logic adapter that uses the context of the current conversation to alter the decision of what result is returned. This feature is most likely to be added next.

- **learning from humans**
(Yes) ChatterBot does have a readonly mode where it will not learn, however by default the ChatterBot will add to it's known selection of responses as an individual communicates with it. It maintains the context of what the statement was and what it was in response to. It also records the number of times a given response has occurred, a useful metric when selecting the most likely response to a given statement.

- **making prediction or/and deduction based on available knowledge**
(Yes) The logic that is currently available is more deduction based, but it just comes down to how each selection is chosen in a given response algorithm. In all cases, the current selection of response algorithms always go with the most likely response.

***

Additionally, creating new response algorithms is fairly simple. To create one, just define a class that completed this interface:

```python
class MyLogicAdapter(LogicAdapter):
    def get(self, text, statement_list, current_conversation):
        # TODO
        return selected_statement
```

Any thoughts on the command name? `wormhole pipe`? maybe with `--generate-code-fd=`, `--accept-code-fd=`, `--in-fd=` and `--out-fd=`?

Hm, I'm not sure control over the in/out fds is useful. The shell pipeline to use this manually would be pretty complicated, so it may be ok to reserve this mode for use by programs.

pipe-mode is pretty symmetric, but if you aren't externally-generating the codes, then there's still one end that creates the code, and the other accepts it. I guess if we wanted the accepting end to enable tab-completion, then we need some sort of distinction between where the data goes and where the interactive code-entry conversation happens.

BTW, Wormhole will eventually make a distinction between "narrow" and "wide" mode, where "wide" is really just the Transit object (so a direct connection if possible). "narrow" mode is entirely store-and-forward on the Rendezvous server. For pipe-mode, we might want narrow mode (because it get set up sooner), or we might want wide mode (because it will exchange data faster, and is not so limited in total data transferred). The Rendezvous server may enforce limits on the total number of messages that can be sent, or their total size, because that mode costs more than the Transit-facilitated transfers. So we might need a `--wide` option too, and some docs on the limits imposed by not opting into it. (For a few kilobytes, the Rendezvous server is fine, but I want bulk transfers to use Transit, and with pipe mode there's no obvious way of knowing which it will be).

Hm, OTOH, maybe the rule is that pipe mode uses narrow wormholes until some threshold of usage is reached, then pauses to switch over to wide mode. The `--wide` flag could switch immediately, if the application knows that it'll need it, which will be faster overall.

Assuming that is the case, unfortunately, your only option will be to manually specify your service limits in an override file or on the command line, as I described in my last comment. My current employer does this for many limits across many regions and accounts, though we have Enterprise support and have opened a feature request to get AWS to expose the rest of the current limits via API. When/if that is ever done, I will add support for it ASAP.

If you do want to upgrade, you would need to install the latest version the same way you installed the version you currently have. If you used pip (``pip install awslimitchecker``), you would just have to re-run that command with the option to upgrade (``pip install --upgrade awslimitchecker``). Once that is done, you can refresh trusted advisor using the pertinent command line arguments; see ``awslimitchecker --help``.

I've answered your stackoverflow question with a solution. For completeness I'll add the example here as well:

```
# vim: set fileencoding=utf-8
import six
import sys
import time
import signal
import multiprocessing
import progressbar


def do_work(number):
    if not number % 50:
        print 'Special log occasion ...'
        sys.stdout.flush()
    time.sleep(0.1)


class IOQueue(six.StringIO):

    '''
    Very poor and simple IO wrapper which only functions for simple print
    statements
    '''

    def __init__(self, queue, *args, **kwargs):
        six.StringIO.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)
        self.queue = queue

    def write(self, value):
        self.queue.put(value)


def example(redirect_stdout):
    workers = multiprocessing.cpu_count()
    num_tasks = 1000
    pbar = progressbar.ProgressBar(
        widgets=[progressbar.Bar()],
        max_value=num_tasks,
        redirect_stdout=redirect_stdout,
    )
    # Start a with SIGINT turned of, so that workers can be interrupted
    original_sigint_handler = signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal.SIG_IGN)

    stdout_queue = multiprocessing.Queue()

    def initializer(queue):
        sys.stdout = IOQueue(queue)

    pool = multiprocessing.Pool(
        processes=workers, initializer=initializer, initargs=[stdout_queue])
    signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, original_sigint_handler)

    for i, _ in enumerate(pool.imap(do_work, xrange(num_tasks)), 1):
        while not stdout_queue.empty():
            sys.stdout.write(stdout_queue.get())

        pbar.update(i)

    pool.close()
    pool.join()
    pbar.finish()

example(True)
```

WebSocketHandlet class : 
`
    def read_http_headers(self):
        headers = {}
        # first line should be HTTP GET
        http_get = self.rfile.readline().decode().strip()
		
		# Next three lines will get your end_point
        if re.search('\/([A-Za-z0-9]+)', http_get).group(1):
            self.with_end_point = re.search('\/([A-Za-z0-9]+)', http_get).group(1)
			
        assert http_get.upper().startswith('GET')
        # remaining should be headers
        while True:
            header = self.rfile.readline().decode().strip()
            if not header:
                break
            head, value = header.split(':', 1)
            headers[head.lower().strip()] = value.strip()
        return headers`

Your change has the following issues, not providing backwards compatibility:
```
Type change: data: IndexedSeq[NDArray] -> data: IndexedSeq[(String, NDArray)]
Default value removed: private val dataBatchSize: Int = 1 -> private val dataBatchSize: Int
Default value removed: shuffle: Boolean = false -> shuffle: Boolean
Default value removed: lastBatchHandle: String = "pad" -> lastBatchHandle: String
Argument removed: dataName: String = "data"
Argument removed: labelName: String = "label"
```

Backwards compatibility means that users using version 1.0 of MXNet, should not receive any errors when they upgrade to version 1.X. In your PR, you're modifying the constructor. This would throw an error for all users if they upgrade to the latest version. 

Instead, you should add an additional overload of the constructor in order to support the old constructor as well as providing a new one using your proposed method. Speak, don't modify existing constructors but rather create another one.

Both projects are superficially similar, both implement Baidu's DeepSpeech
algorithms. However, the similarities ends there.

Algorithms are far less than half the battle when dealing with STT engines.
There are various other issues one must deal with:

   - Obtaining speech corpora
   - Computational power for training
   - How to allow such models to be used by users
   - How to walk the patent minefield of speech recognition
   - ...

We will purchase and create speech corpora. Our community will help us in
creating speech corpora, targeting the many languages of our community[1
<http://murmur.vaani.io/>]. We will also be able to purchase speech corpora
which are, unfortunately, rather expensive, out of reach of the lone
developer.

In addition we have the computational resources to train the models.
Usually training takes on the order a 1 week of time on a relatively
powerful server, 4 Pascal Titans X's, for even a small data set of 300
hours. So, most people will not be able to train such models. However, we
will train and release such models to the world allowing the community to
profit from our work.

The code in the repo you reference simply trains a model and does not
present any means to use the trained model in a real-world situation. It's
for research only. To use the models one must create a client server
system, which we do, or create an interface to the system for users to
obtain STT results.

Unbeknownst to most there are patent issues involved with running
client/server based speech recognition, patent issues which prevent one
from doing so without licensing a patent. The repo you reference creates a
model that is so large that it can only be server based. Thus, it's
unusable for commercial purposes due to patent issues. We will apply
various techniques to modify our engine so it is able to run on small
footprint devices, think RPi3. Doing so we will skirt patent issues and
allow our STT engine to be used without licensing a patent. In addition
this will guarantee our engine a higher level of security, as it will not
stream speech data across the network.

I actually recently documented mOffsetMatrix more clearly... the previous documentation was wrong. It should be much more clear now. #1803 

the mOffsetMatrix should always be a transformation from the space of the bone to the space of the mesh. So to get a vertex position in world space, you take the bone transform in world space, multiply by mOffsetMatrix, then apply it to the vertex position in mesh space. This should remain the same no matter how the bone is animated.

It's calculated from the world-space transforms of the bone and mesh in the bind pose. The bone transform is inverted, and this is multiplied by the mesh transform, resulting in a transform from bone space to mesh space, via world space.

> what is the benefit of the Google docstring flavor?
Readability, that's the main advantage of it, it's way more readable for a human, ex:
```python
def something(one, two):
    """Does somethnig.

    :param one: first param
    :type one: str
    :param two: second param
    :type two: int
    :retrun: the answer to the most important question of life, the universe and everything.
    :rtype: int
    :rises: TowelNotFound when there's no towel to be found.
    :rises: UnrisableException when it can't rise an exception.
    :example:
        something('ni', 41)
    """

## vs

def something(one, two):
    """Does somethnig.

    Args:
        one (str): first param
        two (int): second param

    Returns:
        int: the answer to the most important question of life, the universe and everything.

    Rises:
        TowelNotFound when there's no towel to be found.
        UnrisableException: when it can't rise an exception.

    Example:
        something('ni', 41)
    """
```

And according to @jacquerie it will also complain when a parameter is missing in the docstring or when a parameter in the docstring is not in the function signature anymore (helping getting the docs in sync with the code).

> Why has it been chosen?
Because we did a technical meeting to decide about documentation processes and standards, and that's what we did :) (we can discuss it on another meeting if you want to discuss it further).

> How does it play WRT the parallel sprint made by upstream Invenio devs on finalizing documentation for all invenio modules?
It's totally independent on our side, we will still use as many docs from invenio as we can (to avoid duplicating work) but all the other aspects are only inspire-side.

```python
import pygit2
bool(pygit2.features & pygit2.GIT_FEATURE_HTTPS)
bool(pygit2.features & pygit2.GIT_FEATURE_SSH)
```

@taion An explicit `pipenv lock` already fully resolves both `[packages]` and `[dev-packages]`, so we shouldn't need to worry about configuration drift in that case (and that's the case where I think it's most desirable to be detecting dependency conflicts).

If there's a symmetric solution that also allows both `pipenv install package` and `pipenv install --dev package` to detect (and resolve) inconsistencies, then I agree that would be a good way to go. However, I'd also being OK with an asymmetric solution, if that was easier to implement (hence the framing of this proposal).

For the symmetric approach, I think one way of framing & implementing that would be to pursue the approach that @vphilippon described in https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/966#issuecomment-339791934, but structure it as follows:

* add a `pipenv lock --keep-outdated` option to request a minimal lockfile update rather than the default comprehensive one
* change `pipenv install package` to be defined as "edit Pipfile -> `pipenv lock --keep-outdated` -> `pipenv install`"

(Note: for security management reasons, I *really* want to keep the default behaviour of `pipenv lock` as upgrading everything - however, I do think a feature like `--keep-outdated` has a place when other mechanisms are in place to ensure timely responses to reported security issues in dependencies)

I think a simple fix would be `if q.unit.physical_type == 'angle' or 'pix' in q.unit.name: `

This is probably a problem of astropy. We can reproduce the problem in a fairly easy way:
```
In [1]: import numpy as np
In [2]: import astropy.units as u

In [3]: dist_GC = 8.5 * u.kpc
In [4]: dist_GC.unit is u.kpc
Out[4]: True

In [5]: w = dist_GC ** 2
In [6]: w.unit
Out[6]: Unit("kpc2")

In [7]: x = np.sqrt(w)
In [8]: x.unit
Out[8]: Unit("kpc") # this is correct

In [9]: x.unit is u.kpc
Out[9]: False # but the assertion here fails.
```

When `SkyEllipticalAperture` calculates the semi-major/minor axes, it takes the square root of the first/second eigenvalues of the covariance matrix (which has units like `pix2`). The units are correctly propagated after `numpy.sqrt`, but the assertion of  `q.unit is u.pixel` would fail.

Agreed, incremental builds from date filters have this general issue at present (this has been an issue for a long time). I've thought a bit about how to make this more robust, but haven't implemented anything yet.  One thought I've had would be to build in a temporary staging collection and then copy to the target collection, which would presumably only fail if the copying step was interrupted (rather than the process_item->update targets pipeline).  This puts a bit more of a space load on the database, though, and could still fail if the final copying step is interrupted.

I think there's probably a more effective strategy via some additional logic in the build bookkeeping or a validation step, perhaps all of the new documents in a collection have an "incremental_validation" field or something that only gets added collection-wide when an incremental build completes.

I just did a AWS Lambda function. I didn't use this package at all. The configuration in python-dockerflow is Django-specific, so that won't fly for Lambda (unless you're doing crazy things).

The logging bits might be helpful, but logs for AWS Lambda go to Cloudwatch, so for it to be really helpful, we'd have to have something that slurps from Cloudwatch and into our logging infrastructure.

Also, if you're using Datadog, then you print the statsd data to stdout and Datadog picks it up by reading Cloudwatch. You can't have that in mozlog format.

I have some rough infrastructure in Pigeon for configuration, logging, and statsd things if that helps. I'd be game for pulling that into python-dockerflow if that helps:

> The only way this would work is if I had done something like from numpy import array (and if I do that, then I get an error from another part of the repr which uses dtype=float32, which also hasn't been defined).

Yes, that is exactly what you need to do.  Also typically `from numpy import float32, ...`.  The point is not that `eval(repr(obj))` will always work in all situations, but rather that there is at least some state in which it will work.  That means that it is a complete, unambiguous description of the object with no missing details.

We would never advertise `eval(repr(obj))` as being a good way to reconstruct GalSim objects.

I'm starting to think that maybe the simplest way to distinguish the SED'd from the non-SED'd ChromaticObjects is to give every ChromaticObject an SED, but track the dimensions (really, units) of the SED.  (Similar to @dkirkby 's suggestion of using astropy.units to specify profile size parameters [here](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/issues/657)).  Specifically, the `wave_type` and `flux_type` arguments to `SED.__init__` could be, in addition to a string, an `astropy.units.core.UnitBase` instance.

We could then propagate the units when multiplying `SED`s and `Bandpass`es and raise an exception if the resulting dimensions are not either [1] or [photons / wavelength-interval / area / time].  (Maybe also allow dimensions with energy <-> photons or wavelength-interval <-> frequency-interval).  We can still only permit `drawImage(bandpass)` and other restrictions if the dimensions make sense.

Pros:
  - simpler ChromaticObject implementation
  - no need for multiple inheritance or multiple versions of `ChromaticConvolution`.

Cons:
  - astropy becomes mandatory (at least for `ChromaticObject`s)
  - `SED` becomes a bit more of a misnomer (although we're already storing the photon distribution and not the energy distribution, so maybe this is okay.)
  - A more complicated `SED.__init__` API, though I think this may still be backwards compatible with existing options for `wave_type` and `flux_type`.

So, I'm specifically working on this.  It seems like there's two potential pathways forwards:

1. write to the shared information service (SIS) with the information about the manual failover
2. send signals to the API on each node about the failover

SIS Appoach:
Advantages:
* client only needs to access the SIS
* easy to integrate with SIS-based monitoring
* consistency with other signals we may want to pass via the SIS, i.e. "nogovernor" flag
* allows signalling other replicas not to try to grab the master flag
* requires only a very simple client script

Disadvantages:
* very asynchronous; hard to know when failover is complete except by polling
* zero troubleshooting info if failover doesn't happen
* requires admins writing to the SIS, which is a new pattern

Node API approach
Advantages:
* synchronous: can find out how failover is going immediately
* doesn't require touching the main failover loop
* makes targeting specific nodes fairly obvious

Disadvangates:
* requires securing the node API because now you can change databases via it.
* client would need to be heavy; will have to connect to all database nodes, possibly, to make the failover go as planned
* possible synchronization issues between data in SIS and what individual nodes are doing

Overall, it seems to me that doing this via the SIS makes more sense.  Discussion?

#### Creating a tax benefit system.
A tax benefit system is a set of :
* Variables (some of them calculable)
* Entities for which these variables are defined
* Legislation parameters. 

**Example**
``` py

# This hasn't change: I need to create my entities and scenario.

class Scenario(AbstractScenario):
	#...

class Familles(AbstractEntity):
	#...

class Individus(AbstractEntity):
	#...


# Variable classes can still inherit Variable, DatedVariable, EntityToPersonColumn or PersonToEntityColumn.
class age_en_mois(Variable):
	#...

class birth(Variable):
	#...

class salaire_brut(Variable):
	#...

# I want to define a particular tax benefit system.
class TestTaxBenefitSystem(TaxBenefitSystem):

	# The construction of the TBS is done in its constructor.
    def __init__(self):

    	# I first need to call the parent constructor for a basic initialization, providing the entities in a simple list.
        entities = [Familles, Individus]
        TaxBenefitSystem.__init__(self, entities)

        self.Scenario = Scenario

        # I add a variable to the TBS
        self.add_variable(age_en_mois)

        # I add several variables at the same time.
        self.add_variables(birth, salaire_brut)

        # I add all the ones declared in a python file
        self.add_variables_from_file("/path/to/openfisca_variables.py")

        # I recursively browse a directory to add all the variables declared in it.
        self.add_variables_from_directory("other/path/to/openfisca/variables/")

        # I declare the main parameter file
        path_to_root_params = 'path/to/root_params.xml'
        self.add_legislation_params(path_to_root_params)

        # I add another parameter file
		path_to_crds_params = 'path/to/crds_params.xml'
		# It will be added to the tree into the node `csg.activite`
        self.add_legislation_params(path_to_crds_params, 'csg.activite')
```

For anyone else needing a workaround, I was able to use callables within the model for the index.

```
class Comment(models.Model):
    user = models.ForeignKey(User)
    raw_message = models.TextField()

    def user_filter(self):
        return [self.user.username]
```

Then the index:

```
class CommentIndex(AlgoliaIndex):
    fields = ('raw_message')
    tags = 'user_filter'
```

This isn't perfect and may not work in all situations, but it seems to be sufficient when you need to manage user access with [secured API keys](https://www.algolia.com/doc/python#security--user-api-keys).

`list.append` and `list.pop` are atomic operations, which means that the list won't be corrupted by separate threads calling `_push_connection`. I am also having an issue with speed with queries to the datastore from HTTP trigger functions.

It isn't limited to running queries though. Making http requests using the "request" module takes a long time too.

I don't think it's a matter of "cold starts", caching, authentication, or logging/other activities done during requests that could slow it down. I've searched around and saw many people having similar issues but none coming to solutions. Many suggestions such as the aforementioned but no solutions to the core issue.

I could understand a 500ms or even a second, but it takes many seconds...sometimes 10+ seconds datastore queries (very small dataset) and HTTP requests to external APIs. I've searched and I think the following people had issues maybe not exactly the same, but which probably have a root cause:
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-node/issues/2284
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39878311/google-cloud-datastore-runquery-is-extremely-slow-for-app-engine-nodejs-app

https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-node/issues/2374
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40454958/google-cloud-datastore-slow-800ms-with-simple-query-from-compute-engine

I don't think it's the node.js datastore package but instead I think it's related to the infrastructure and/or code base that Node.js Google Cloud HTTP trigger functions,  Node.js Firebase HTTP trigger functions, and Node.js Google Computer Engine applications have in common and use to make HTTP requests in general. Something that they have in common is causing slow HTTP requests to be made, I think.

```
			except http.client.IncompleteRead as e:
				html = BeautifulSoup(e.partial, 'lxml')
			allRows = html.find('table', {'class':'tblchart'}).find_all('tr')
			if len(allRows[2].find_all('td')) < 6:
				#the data ended!
				break
			else: 
				del allRows[:2]
				for row in allRows:
					col = row.find_all('td')
					tbl = pd.DataFrame([{'date':pd.to_datetime(col[0].get_text(), format = "%d-%m-%Y"), 'open':col[1].get_text(), 'high':col[2].get_text(), 'low':col[3].get_text(), 'close':col[4].get_text(), 'volume':col[5].get_text()}])
					result = result.append(tbl,ignore_index =False)
		pn+=1
	result = result.set_index(keys = ['date'])
	result['script_id'] = script_id
	if pn>1:
		return result
	else:
		return url1

def writeStockDfToDb(data, engine = engine, tblName = 'scripts_eod_data'):
	connection = engine.connect()
	data.to_sql(name = tblName, con= engine, index= True, if_exists='append')
 ```

There is a janky global function you can define to intercept the final options object before it is sent to the ``dataTable()`` constructor, but it's getting dropped in the upcoming release in favor of avoiding automatic initialization.

Conceivably you could modify the ``datatableview.defaults`` object to accomplish this same thing across all tables, if you need to have control over it per initialization, sending it explicitly will be the official way to go.

To make it clearer where one might change this if they look at the code, I'm adding a global default for sorting.  Searching is different because dataTables.js has you specify it per column; you could set a different ``sDom`` value to get rid of the search field entirely.  All of the settings available to you in dataTables.js are allowed in the the ``datatableview.defaults`` or during manual initialization, since they're just being forwarded to the library.  If you have questions about any of those settings, I'd recommend their documentation.

@filipeximenes - I thought about this and I think we can add a small number of parameters (and only one, if it's acceptable to force utf-8) instead of using this catch-all approach to address your concern.

After reviewing, we probably won't need too much beyond `full_document` and possibly `encoding`. As written, this pr converts to utf-8 anyway as a catch-all for Python 2 vs 3 issues.

-----
The story behind `xmltodict_unparse__`:

Opting to give the developer full control of `parse()` & `unparse()` (instead of supplying a boolean mapped to the `full_document` param of `unparse()`) was a choice to eliminate a potential blocker for wrapper developers. I'm not sure about the full range of XML requirements, but assume that `xmltodict` has relevant options.

The original requirement I encountered was to remove the XML declaration (example: `<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no" ?>`) of requests to the API I was testing with. Since different APIs will have different requirements, we shouldn't hard-code it.

```
			self.send_stream_Pyobj.terminate()
		except:
			print "Send audio stream and object doesn't exist"

		# close receive audio stream and thread
		try:
			self.stream_receive.stop_stream()
			self.stream_receive.close()
			self.receive_stream_Pyobj.terminate()
			self.play_audio_received_thread.stopped=True
		except:
			print "Receive audio stream and object doesn't exist"

		


class AudioClientFactory(ClientFactory):
	def __init__(self, canvas_obj,username):
		self.canvas_obj = canvas_obj
		self.username=username

	def buildProtocol(self, addr):
		self.protocol=AudioClient(self,self.canvas_obj)
		return self.protocol

					
if __name__ == '__main__':
	from twisted.internet import reactor
	reactor.connectTCP("127.0.0.1",3036,AudioClientFactory('junkcanvas_obj','something'))
	reactor.run()
```

the first short example:

`from flowtracks.trajectory import Trajectory

def trajectories_trackpy(data):
    """
    Converts all trajectories from a trackpy dataframe to the flowtracks trajectory format
    
    Arguments:
    data - trackpy data, pandas DataFrame with a column 'particle','frame', x','y'
    
    
    Returns:
    trajects - a list of :class:`~flowtracks.trajectory.Trajectory` objects,
        one for each trajectory contained in the trackpy data.
    """
    
    raw = data.groupby('particle')
    
    trajects = []
    for name, group in raw:
        # name is an id
        # group has ['x'],['y'] columns
        # also convert data from mm to m.
        pos = np.vstack((group.x, group.y, 0*group.x)).T # in pixels
        dvx = np.gradient(group.x)
        dvy = np.gradient(group.y)
        vel = np.vstack((dvx, dvy, 0*dvx)).T # in pix/dt
        ax = np.gradient(dvx)
        ay = np.gradient(dvy)
        accel = np.vstack((np.gradient(dvx), np.gradient(dvy), 0*dvx)).T
        t = group.frame
        trajid = np.int(name)
        trajects.append(Trajectory(pos, vel, t, trajid, accel=accel))
    
    return trajects`

It's a good question. Porting controller to py3 would be a bit of work, and in the other two cases where this shows up in the build it's not a Deis project directly (ceph-monitor, docker-registry). And sadly today's state of affairs is that py2 is still heavily preferred everywhere, despite our efforts to move things forward.

We could do it for the controller, but we might need to make the whole enchilada py2/3 compatible at the same time, or else require devs to install python3 in order to do `make -C controller/ test-unit` and the like. It's not totally unreasonable, but every time something like this comes up ("let's use Django 1.~7~8"), we find we would rather start rewriting things in Go if we're going to do anything disruptive.

(And having said all that, this is just a warning. Based on my reading of it, Deis probably doesn't trigger the actual security bugs referenced.)

This is happening because some other Qt version is getting picked up before Anaconda's.  Neither the Anaconda prompt nor IPython from the start menu have issues because they are prepending entries to PATH for their processes.  This is done when activating environments, so any activated environment should also not have issues.

For the root environment, you have two options:
1. Hack (root installation)/Lib/site.py (see https://groups.google.com/a/continuum.io/forum/#!searchin/anaconda/pyqt$20dll$20error/anaconda/vcY-P7MBwo0/hQByFQsNAwAJ)
2. Move the Anaconda paths in your system PATH environment variable earlier.

1 is much less invasive.  This change has been made in our source code, but the package hasn't been released yet.

By the way: a clear indicator that some other Qt is getting picked up is the Windows 10 warning.  That was making our tests fail, and we have disabled it.  Seeing that message absolutely means "this is the wrong Qt dll."

I think even now, people just remove the feature metapackage by name. So for example, `conda remove nomkl` does the same thing as `conda remove --features nomkl`, because there is a one-to-one correspondence between that track feature and a package. In fact as you'll see in my next commit, I think the best we can do is depend on that correspondence.

Did you add Anaconda to your PATH? It's likely pkg-config that's causing
you this trouble if so. Also since cmake has its own scripting language,
build system authors are free to rummage around your PATH (or other env
vars) looking for things if they so choose.

My opinion is that the activate and deactivate shell scripts should be used
instead of adding Anaconda to your PATH.

> The idea is to differentiate between requested packages and those installed solely as dependencies. We'd then allow conda to remove those "dependency" packages if necessary to accomplish upgrade goals. Of course, we'd always minimize the number of such removals, but in a case like this, it seems like it would be worthwhile.

👍 Love this idea.

So, I'm guessing requested means one did `conda install <X>` and now `<X>` is added to requested column, but its dependency `<Y>` is not. Doesn't `yum` do something similar? Anyways this sounds like a very good idea to me.

> Here's a simple option: build empty versions of these "compatibility" packages.

@ijstokes I believe this is fixed with 4.1.0rc2. Please reopen if you run into it again.

I've been thinking about this more and I think there is some good news here. As long as we do the following two things, we'll be fine:

- Remove the `vc*` features from python
- Replace `vc*` dependencies with direct dependency runtimes for any non-Python package, like git.

The realization is this: this effectively makes the `vc` features _redundant and useless_. But that's fine, because the only thing we _really_ need here is _exclusivity_. That is: we need to make sure that only one `vc*` dependency is employed by all Python extensions. The solution generated by conda 4.3.30 above, which has _both_ `vc14` and `vc9` features present, is caused by the fact that `python` has a track-feature when it shouldn't. Perform the first fix above, and that problem goes away.

For this we have the `conda clean --packages` command.  Effectively adding a callback to `conda clean --packages` after a `conda update` command would be a major change in behavior at this point.  Many conda users rely on the package cache being populated for (1) usage with the `--offline` flag and (2) the ability to roll back to previous installs with `conda list --revisions` and `conda install --rev REV_NUM`.

If there is more interest in the future by more users, we can continue to discuss.  For now, I'm going to close this feature request as "won't fix" given the above explanation.

Right now, the way we've told people to manage this is to install a separate Miniconda for the platform they are interested in (at least for the 32-, 64-bit issue). I'm not sure if it's worth the complexity that would be added to conda to make things work the way you say. A lot of places in the code assume there is a single platform, and that that platform matches `sys.platform`. And as you note, there is also the assumption that there only needs to be one package cache, one index, etc. 

Regarding use-case 2, that might not really work in general anyway, since conda packages in general cannot be relocated once they are installed, because hard-coded paths in files are replaced with the install prefix at install time. 

At any rate, the use-case 1 seems legitimate, but I don't really see the value of allowing to install packages for a completely different operating system.

Ok, it definitely took a few months longer to get back to this than I expected - sorry about that!

As a sanity check, can I ask why this is a feature you find valuable?  I'm not necessarily opposed to adding this in, but I'm generally wary since more features and complexity are dangerous because they increase the likelihood of bugs creeping in.  That's obviously not enough of a reason to reject a new feature obviously (or else we'd never add new features to anything), but it does make me want to at least do a little due diligence on why this is even being suggested.

Aside from that, one issue that I have with this is that the callback is only passed the section name as a string, which is ambiguous since there can exist different subsections with different names in a deeply-nested ``ConfigObj`` dictionary:

```python
>>> from __future__ import print_function
>>> import configobj
>>> c = configobj.ConfigObj(on_change=lambda *args: print('on_change', args))
>>> c['foo'] = {'foo': {'bar': 'baz'}, 'spam': 'eggs'}
on_change ('foo', 'bar', None, 'baz')
on_change ('foo', 'foo', None, {'bar': 'baz'})
on_change ('foo', 'spam', None, 'eggs')
on_change (None, 'foo', None, {'foo': {'bar': 'baz'}, 'spam': 'eggs'})
>>> c
ConfigObj({'foo': {'spam': 'eggs', 'foo': {'bar': 'baz'}}})
>>> c['foo']['spam'] = 'terrible'
on_change ('foo', 'spam', 'eggs', 'terrible')
```

Note how in the final line above, we only know that the ``foo`` section was changed but do not know which ``foo`` section.  It would probably be better to pass the ``Section`` object itself, which has a ``.name`` attribute which could be used to get the name if the callback wished, so it's not any less convenient.  And then if necessary, the callback can check the ``.parent`` attribute to determine its place in the hierarchy.

I'm also concerned about the fact that we get unexpected behavior when setting the ``on_change`` callback after initialization:

```python
>>> c = configobj.ConfigObj()
>>> c['foo'] = {'foo': {'bar': 'baz'}, 'spam': 'eggs'}
>>> c.on_change = lambda *args: print('on_change', args)
>>> c['foo']['spam'] = 'terrible'
>>> c['hello'] = 'world'
on_change (None, 'hello', None, 'world')
```

Note that in the above example, the callback is invoked ONLY for the top-level dictionary and not for any of the sections.  This seems like it would lead to subtle bugs.  We would need to either disable it from being set after initialization or propagate it to all subsections.  (This raises a further question about what happens if we set a handler on a sub-section; it should probably propagate to its own subsections but not to its ancestors.)

@mcwienczek got an solution:

```
# Create a KeyVault
az keyvault create \
  --name ${ENV_NAME}-keyvault \
  --resource-group ${ENV_NAME} \
  --location ${AZURE_LOCATION} \
  --enabled-for-template-deployment true

# Due to KeyVault DNS propagation, we have to wait for 15 sec
while [[ -z "$(az keyvault show -n ${ENV_NAME}-keyvault -g ${ENV_NAME})" ]]; do
  sleep 0.15
  echo "Waiting for 15 seconds for KeyVault DNS propagation"
done

# Create a secrete for Github token in KeyVault
az keyvault secret set \
  --vault-name ${ENV_NAME}-keyvault \
  --name "GIT-TOKEN" \
  --value "${GIT_TOKEN}"

```

Closing per @praveenbarli request.

CLI does't cache the token for the following reasons 
1. MSI token response doesn't have the refresh token, so the token can't be used beyond 1 hour. For any dependent parties to behave reliably, they need to invoke `az login --msi` every hour which could be awkward if in the middle of long running operation.
2. The MSI extension serve token requests at localhost:50342 through very simple protocol, e.g.  `curl -H "Metadata:true" -H "Content-type:application/json"  'http://localhost:50342/oauth2/token?resource=https%3A%2F%2Fmanagement.azure.com%2F'
`. 
3. All Azure SDK have released MSI related authentication classes which have encapsulated such logics for client applications to leverage.

With that, I believe you have much less needs to read the raw token file, which is not meant to be shared  by 3rd party applications.  But please let me know your situation if you do need the raw token file.

```python
def defvjp(self, vjpfun, argnum=0):

    def make_vjp(ans, vs, gvs, *args, **kwargs):

        return vjp
```

@vierja The reason for the discrepancy is that _enter_variable_scope vs defining submodules in _build only unifies the variable_scopes entered, meaning that the variables have the same name whichever way round you do it. However, every time you enter the variable_scope you get a new name scope which will be uniquified if necessary by adding _N. The Tensorboard graph visualises namescopes, rather than variable scopes per se, although there is obviously overlap.

In your second example, the variables (and the ops associated with them - read, assign, init etc) are inside the first time you enter name_scope, which is `main_module` at the bottom. When you call _build, you reenter a name_scope with the same name and TF uniquifies it to `main_module_1`, and you see the add and matmul inside the smaller block up top. If you connect the module a second time you would get another small block, `main_module_2`, with again just a Matmul and Add inside. Similarly, if you connect the module a second as you have it written in the first module, you also get a second block as the name_scope is entered a second time.

I don't think it's possible to change this name_scope behaviour without some hacking of TF, and I don't think that would necessarily be desirable - when connecting modules multiple times you probably want that to show as separate name_scopes in Tensorboard. Otherwise, a module shared lots of times would end up looking extremely confusing as all the input / output connections go through one block.

I hope this explanation helps - please let me know if you have other comments or questions.

> However, my point still stands. I'm not 100% sure about Kallisto, but other alignment-free quantifiers (thinking of Sailfish, for a sec) are two-pass (Index then Quantify). How do we accommodate those tools? Do we? Do we force them into a single Job?

IMO, if the indexing step has different input requirements than the tool (like in Kallisto's case), they are separate job functions.  This is just an example, because people are going to have opinions on return format (here I'm returning a FileStoreID of a tarball with Kallisto's outputs), and whether or not arguments should always be FileStoreIDs and not something like a URL.  Sometimes it's convenient to roll steps into one functions — like a simple `faidx` if the .fai file isn't present and which doesn't require any other inputs.

```python
def run_kallisto(job, cores, r1_id, r2_id, kallisto_index):
    """
    RNA quantification via Kallisto

    :param JobFunctionWrappingJob job: passed automatically by Toil
    :param int cores: Number of cores to run Kallisto with
    :param str r1_id: FileStoreID of fastq (pair 1)
    :param str r2_id: FileStoreID of fastq (pair 2 if applicable, otherwise pass None for single-end)
    :param str kallisto_index: FileStoreID for Kallisto index file
    :return: FileStoreID from Kallisto output
    :rtype: str
    """
    work_dir = job.fileStore.getLocalTempDir()
    # Retrieve files
    parameters = ['quant',
                  '-i', '/data/kallisto.idx',
                  '-t', str(cores),
                  '-o', '/data/',
                  '-b', '100']
    job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(kallisto_index, os.path.join(work_dir, "kallisto.idx"))
    if r1_id and r2_id:
        job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(r1_id, os.path.join(work_dir, 'R1_cutadapt.fastq'))
        job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(r2_id, os.path.join(work_dir, 'R2_cutadapt.fastq'))
        parameters.extend(['/data/R1_cutadapt.fastq', '/data/R2_cutadapt.fastq'])
    else:
        job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(r1_id, os.path.join(work_dir, 'R1_cutadapt.fastq'))
        parameters.extend(['--single', '-l', '200', '-s', '15', '/data/R1_cutadapt.fastq'])
    # Call: Kallisto
    docker_call(tool='quay.io/ucsc_cgl/kallisto:0.42.4--35ac87df5b21a8e8e8d159f26864ac1e1db8cf86',
                work_dir=work_dir, parameters=parameters)
    # Tar output files together and store in fileStore
    output_files = [os.path.join(work_dir, x) for x in ['run_info.json', 'abundance.tsv', 'abundance.h5']]
    tarball_files(tar_name='kallisto.tar.gz', file_paths=output_files, output_dir=work_dir)
    return job.fileStore.writeGlobalFile(os.path.join(work_dir, 'kallisto.tar.gz'))
```

@MAFLO321, have you figured out the answer to your question? If not, I'm curious what difference between `run` and `certonly` you're referring to.

Assuming you're not using your own CSR, `run` is basically `certonly` followed by the extra behavior of installing the certificates, performing optional security enhancements, etc.

`--print-key-file` doesn't sound like a useful plugin-like integration. Users see the certificate path once its issued in `letsencrypt auth` command and they can just copy-paste it. Let's Encrypt plugins are a bit more complicated conceptually...

Authenticator plugins are meant to perform challenges, which requires interaction with the webserver configuration (in order to have no downtimes). This doesn't have to involve config file updates - you could provide API on the webserver side to update configuration "on the fly" (in memory), e.g. `h2octl add-new-letsencrypt-dvsni-challenge-host --key foo --cert bar ...` and then create a python plugin that exploits this API. Otherwise, people have to stop the webserver (at least for port 443), use `letsencrypt auth` and afterwards set the paths to the issued certificates manually,

Installer plugins (`letsencrypt install`) are passed certificate paths and it's up to them to decide how to interact with the webserver. You could for example put certificates and keys into predefined directory structure, say `/etc/h20/letsencrypt/$domain/{key,cert}.pem`, and code the server, so that it defaults to reading those files if no key/cert for SSL was provided. This wouldn't require any interaction with the config files.

As you can see, there are other ways than changing the configuration files directly. Use your imagination! :)

This one is a bit weird partially because apparently it's choosing the wrong thing for the hour, but I think at the moment it would be quite hard to support this kind of string, mostly because we don't necessarily have a way to distinguish between `1237` and `2017` as the year (though in this case we *could* theoretically distinguish it because October 30, 1237 was a Thursday).

In this case, since you know the format, I recommend doing something like this:

```python
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil import tz

def parse_specific_string(dtstr):
    dt = datetime.strptime(dtstr, '%H%M %p %Z %a %b %d %Y')
    tzname = dtstr[8:].split(' ', 1)[0]
    if tzname:
        tzi = tz.gettz(tzname)
        dt = dt.replace(tzinfo=tzi)

    return dt
```

I would then take the person who decided to use `'%H%M %p %Z %a %b %d %Y'` as a string format and give them a stern talking-to. I think `%Z` may only be "supported" for Python 3, and even then it really just ignores that section of the string. No matter how you slice it, though, it's risky business using the 3-letter time zone name abbreviations to actually get a time zone name.

Below is the piece of code used to produce `hapt_data.sframe` from the raw data found [here](http://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/00341/HAPT%20Data%20Set.zip).
This follows the instructions in the README.txt file available with the raw data.

```python
from glob import glob
import turicreate as tc

def find_label_for_containing_interval(intervals, index):
    containing_interval = intervals[:, 0][(intervals[:, 1] <= index) & (index <= intervals[:, 2])]
    if len(containing_interval) == 1:
        return containing_interval[0]

# Load labels
labels = tc.SFrame.read_csv('./HAPT Data Set/RawData/labels.txt', delimiter=' ', header=False, verbose=False)
labels = labels.rename({'X1': 'exp_id', 'X2': 'user_id', 'X3': 'activity_id', 'X4': 'start', 'X5': 'end'})

# Load data
data = tc.SFrame()
acc_files = glob('./HAPT Data Set/RawData/acc_*.txt')
gyro_files = glob('./HAPT Data Set/RawData/gyro_*.txt')
files = zip(sorted(acc_files), sorted(gyro_files))
for acc_file, gyro_file in files:
    exp_id = int(acc_file.split('_')[1][-2:])
    user_id = int(acc_file.split('_')[2][4:6])
    
    # Load accel data
    sf = tc.SFrame.read_csv(acc_file, delimiter=' ', header=False, verbose=False)
    sf = sf.rename({'X1': 'acc_x', 'X2': 'acc_y', 'X3': 'acc_z'})
    sf['exp_id'] = exp_id
    sf['user_id'] = user_id
    
    # Load gyro data
    gyro_sf = tc.SFrame.read_csv(gyro_file, delimiter=' ', header=False, verbose=False)
    gyro_sf = gyro_sf.rename({'X1': 'gyro_x', 'X2': 'gyro_y', 'X3': 'gyro_z'})
    sf = sf.add_columns(gyro_sf)
    
    # Calc labels
    exp_labels = labels[labels['exp_id'] == exp_id][['activity_id', 'start', 'end']].to_numpy()
    sf = sf.add_row_number()
    sf['activity_id'] = sf['id'].apply(lambda x: find_label_for_containing_interval(exp_labels, x))
    sf = sf.remove_columns(['id', 'exp_id'])
    
    data = data.append(sf)

target_map = {
    1.: 'walking',          
    2.: 'climbing_upstairs',
    3.: 'climbing_downstairs',
    4.: 'sitting',
    5.: 'standing',
    6.: 'laying'
}

# Use the same labels used in the experiment
data = data.filter_by(target_map.keys(), 'activity_id')
data['activity'] = data['activity_id'].apply(lambda x: target_map[x])
data = data.remove_column('activity_id')

data.save('hapt_data.sframe')
```

@wintersteiger 
> Right, that's all good reasons. It's quite possible that /linkresource behaves differently in/via mono, I never looked into that.

Under mono the both build systems do not pass the ``/linkresource`` flag because Mono will look for ``libz3.so`` in the standard search paths when using the .NET bindings. If the ``libz3.so`` is installed the library is found and if a user needs to use the library in the build directory ``LD_LIBRARY_PATH`` can be set appropriately.

> We don't publish installers for Windows anymore, but only .zip files for each release which contain everything in the default folders and it's just generally likely that those files will always be in the same directory anyways (at least on Windows). Actual "installation" in the case of .NET libraries may also mean that the libraries are added to the GAC, which, in the past, very few people needed, but for those few it was a bit of a headache.

It looks like under Windows the ``gacutil`` doesn't have the ``-root`` flag like mono does so there isn't a way to install outside of the system's GAC which would make distributing the tarball of ``make install DESTDIR=/temp/path/to/tarball`` containing a partial GAC impossible. Under Windows it might just make more sense to copy ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` into ``<prefix>/lib`` instead.

This isn't really an issue yet as you're not using CMake to create release tarballs so I'm inclined to leave this for now until it actually becomes a problem.

Generally you will have done a scan dir before opening a file, so you know in advance a path is file and not a directory. So its safe to disable strict mode.

Why do you want to use them? Highly NOT recommended.

The number are PWM values - the actual values sent out to the servos that drive the vehicle. So you'll have the normal RC values coming in from your receiver and going into the autopilot. Then the autopilot checks the channel and if you have an override sends the value you specify above instead. 

The channel numbers are (I believe) as you say "by default", but these can be changed by the autopilot. Normally they won't be. 

This just fakes an RC input. So if you send updates with the same PWM values that  you would require to live off as you have said above, that's what it will do. The only caveat is that the autopilot can only respond to messages at a certain rate. 

I'm not going to update the docs - we really don't want people using this feature.

@rajanandakumar Yes the way that this is presenting for LHCb people is that after the offline job splitting has decided how to split a large dataset then Ganga seriously struggles to get to the state of actually submitting a subjob.


In short I suspect this is related to a bug in the fact that we have many files in the GangaXML repo and that Ganga in it's current state is being a little paranoid in writing out too much data when it's not required.

This also explains why the slow-down strongly correlates to large jobs as smaller jobs don't see the same amount of wasted time even as a fraction of their job size.

I've a fix in my private repo that I'm testing and profiling now but as this has taken 2 days just to get to this stage I thought it wise to open a Task about it.

FYI:

Apologies the message is below is a little long so bare with me.


There are a number of different (possible) reasons for this slow down and I think I'll list some of the work I've done on this.

1) Watching ```ganga --debug myscript.py```

1a) flush operations

This reveals that there is a LOT of flush() calls to the large repository which are potentially extremely expensive operations.
1 flush operation for a job with 600 subjobs ends up causing some 1,200 files to be touched on disk!
(not ideal but this is part of the cost of having the repo structured the way that it is)

I've now implemented some logic privately which effects the automatic flushing of the repository.
The fix is to prevent automatically flushing the repository less than once every 10sec.
Of course operations such as shutdown etc. all call the flush function explicitly and will still flush the repo when this is automatically called. I'm referring to the flush operations happen due to an object being marked dirty.

This is related to the ```GangaList``` problem I've fixed and spoken about recently where adding 10 objects to a ```GangaList``` would trigger a repo flush. This causes a problem when I'm adding many objects to a ```GangaList``` such as LFNs.

1b) flushing a job flushes the whole job, every time
Another problem this highlighted is that the repository ONLY knows about the master job and when it's marked dirty it flushes all objects which have it as a parent to the XML repo. (This includes ALL subjobs as far as I can tell and is an obvious area where we can improve. No need to load a job to disk just to re-write it unchanged and no need to flush a job that hasn't been altered in memory)

Aka, when I submit subjob 1 I don't want to be forced to modify the on-disk details for subjobs 2-600.


2) Reading the code/diffs

In checking the code between the various versions I spotted a minor regression at the end of the ```SplitByFiles``` code for LHCb. This has been fixed but doesn't gain too much performance improvement. Although it does speed up the actual creation of the subjob ```Job``` object within this splitter.
I have also spotted a 'bug' in the deepcopy method which will effect large datasets. This effected Ganga v601r14 (aka HEAD). Python makes use of the ```__repr__``` function to determine if a deepcopy operation is reaching an infinite loop and abort. This string operation in early 6.1.14 (I don't remember if this is in 6.1.13) was tied into a pretty string function which when called many, many times internally was causing a lot of excess CPU to be used.

3) Profiling

Profiling for LHCb is relatively straight forward as we don't re-execute ```Ganga``` within itself during the bootstrap so we don't have any problems from this.
Profiling the code has revealed that there is a lot of time spend in the deepcopy method which I mentioned above for the ```GangaList``` objects which are used internally.
In addition to this a LOT of internal CPU time it taken up by the ```__set__``` method of the ```GangaObject``` class which appears to be due to copying ```GangaList``` style objects and an excessive amount of flushing to disk happening.
This is a little trickier to make use of but has been helpful in spotting stupid things like when we were calling the Config system some 1x10^6 times during startup/shutdown which has now been significantly reduced by some lookup caching in the right places.

If you want n colors from a qualitative color map, you can do that in Palettable e.g. via:

```
from palettable.colorbrewer.qualitative import Set1_9
colors = Set1_9.colors  # a list of length 9
```

It's tricky to get large palettes that are also color-blind safe however, e.g. see http://mkweb.bcgsc.ca/colorblind/ for some examples of [7 color palettes](http://mkweb.bcgsc.ca/colorblind/img/colorblindness.palettes.trivial.png) and [12 color palettes](http://mkweb.bcgsc.ca/colorblind/img/colorblindness.palettes.simple.png). I don't really think any plot with more than ~7 categorical/qualitative colors will ever look that great though.

>  given a Paletteable string and a number n of colors to return, returns a list of n "equally spaced" hex colors

This is easy enough in Palettable, the `.mpl_colormap` property will return a matplotlib cmap, which then takes values in 0–255 and returns a color, but I wouldn't advise doing this for the above reasons.

But this is just for qualitative/categorical colors. If you need a quantitative, just use viridis courtesy of BIDS :)

## How to install your own Game and States in Retro:

*Note that these instructions are written for version 0.5.4 of retro and are thus likely to break as the repo changes and becomes more stable*

- Install retro: https://github.com/openai/retro
- Obtain the ROM you are interested in; right now only Genesis and Atari 2600 ROMs work
- Make a game folder in <python_install_path>/Lib/site-packages/retro/data
E.g. If we want to make a Street Fighter II instance: name the folder StreetFighterII-Genesis
- Add the ROM to this folder and rename it to rom.md (for Genesis) or rom.a26 (for Atari 2600)
- Download and install the RetroArch emulator: http://www.retroarch.com/?page=platforms
- Take the .dll or .so or whatever from <python_install_path>/Lib/site-packages/retro/cores and put them wherever RetroArch has its cores directory. On Windows, this was: C:\Users\<yourname>\AppData\Roaming\RetroArch\cores. Good luck on other platforms.
- Open RetroArch
    - Navigate to load core->Sega MS/GG/MD/CD (Genesis Plus GX) (or whatever you put in the folder)
For Atari: load core->Atari 2600 (Stella) (or whatever you put in the folder)
    - Now go back to the main menu and scroll to Load Content
    - Find your ROM and load it
    - Play to wherever you want your Agent to be able to start at
    - Save the state using the menu: Command->Save State Options->Save State
    - Take note of which State slot it saved it to - the name of the file will reflect this
    - Do this for all the states you want
    - Close RetroArch
- Find where RetroArch saved your state - on Windows, this will likely be in C:\Users\<yourname>\AppData\Roaming\RetroArch\states - I haven’t checked on other platforms where these files would be located, but my guess is that it is equally obscure
- Move the states (they should end in .state) to the folder you made in Python’s site-packages (with your ROM) and rename them to whatever you want them to be called (but they must end in .state)
- Gzip each state - don’t change its name as part of this - they have to end in .state
- Create a file called “data.json” and put this in it:
```
{
  “info”: {
  }
}
```
- Create a file called “scenario.json” and put this in it:
```
{
}
```
- Create a file called “metadata.json” and put this in it:
```
{
  “default_state”: “Name of the state you want to set to default, minus the .state ending”
}
```

You should now be able to run the example Agent script: https://github.com/openai/retro/blob/master/examples/random_agent.py by passing in the name of your game (E.g. “StreetFighterII-Genesis” and (potentially) the state name “MyState”).

Some of these steps are likely not needed, but I put them here because I don’t want to bother trying out what is and what isn’t important.
