Hi J-Fo-S,

viewing a Hub module's graph(s) is certainly a useful thing to do, but AFAIK, there is no ready-made solution for it. But it shouldn't be too hard to adapt the widely circulated techniques for visualizing GraphDefs in a colab notebook (your code above) or in TensorBoard (see https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/python/tools/import_pb_to_tensorboard.py -- if only somebody contributed SavedModel support right there ;-).

The key thing to note is that the current file format for Hub modules is essentially a SavedModel. So you can `tar xzf` the module, read the `saved_model.pb` file from it and parse it as a SavedModel protocol message (`from tensorflow.core.protobuf import saved_model_pb2`). Then select the MetaGraphDef for the tag set of interest (usually, the empty list `[]`), and pass its GraphDef to one of the existing solutions (cf. https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/python/saved_model/loader_impl.py).

Needless to say, this breaks through multiple API abstractions and comes with *zero guarantees* (future Hub modules might not be SavedModels, future SavedModels might use a different format). However, it should be good enough for an ad-hoc exploration.

BTW, you don't need to worry about big constants inlined into the graph. With a SavedModel, the trained weights go into a checkpoint that is packaged in the `variables` subdirectory and are restored as needed. This is one of the advantages over the old practice of using "frozen GraphDefs".

To provide some context, Dask wraps up some ufuncs from NumPy. One of them is `cbrt`. However `cbrt` is only added in NumPy 1.10. It appears we are installing NumPy 1.9 on AppVeyor (though it doesn't appear to be intentional) hence the `AttributeError`. Ideally Dask should include a lower bound on NumPy. Added PR ( https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/3283 ) upstream to address this. Though the version of Dask on AppVeyor is also quite old (not sure why 🤔).

Given that Dask currently does not have a lower bound, am a little concerned what might happen if a user mixes NumPy 1.6 and Dask for instance. Not sure if they were ever compatible, but wouldn't be surprised to see some other strange exceptions get generated. Would like to just catch them altogether as opposed to catching different possible errors in a piecemeal fashion to avoid reports like these later. Admittedly using `Exception` is not great.

More generally, we may be overly concerned here if we plan on just dropping NumPy 1.9 altogether.

By my understanding `action='store_true'` will supply the value for the argument, not ask you every time. You have a default supplied, so will be that unless -a is specified. 

What it will do is allow you to use it as a standalone flag eg `aws-google-auth -a` vs `aws-google-auth -a true`

Without the 'store_true' this will happen.

```

```

@richleland I've had a chance to take a closer look at SparkPost, and it seems like a great fit for django-anymail (and a great choice as a transactional ESP).

I'd suggest using the [SparkPost python bindings](https://github.com/SparkPost/python-sparkpost) as the basis for an Anymail SparkPost backend, rather than writing directly to your HTTP API, for exactly the maintainability reasons you mentioned. Anymail's base backend/payload are meant to be binding- and transport-agnostic. [I originally went down the official bindings path with one of the other ESPs supported by Anymail, but backed off when it became clear their python bindings weren't actively maintained and were lacking functionality available in their raw API. It doesn't seem like that would be a problem with python-sparkpost.]

That should also help with getting Anymail users immediate access to critical fixes and future SparkPost improvements, as they can update python-sparkpost independently of Anymail releases. Also, based on experiences with Djrill/Mandrill, I'm also trying to design Anymail with some "escape hatches" (like `esp_extra` and `esp_response`) that allow users to directly access ESP API features without waiting for Anymail updates.

Lastly, I'm very open to adding collaborators to this project, and hoping it will live on outside any one organization or individual.

Let me know if I can answer any other questions. As I alluded to earlier, the docs are missing a "how to add an ESP" section right now, but most of it should be pretty straightforward. I'd be thrilled to have SparkPost support in Anymail.

I've temporarily enabled RTD on the feature_inbound branch, and preliminary inbound docs are available at http://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/feature_inbound/inbound/. (The [installation instructions](http://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/feature_inbound/installation/#configuring-tracking-and-inbound-webhooks) have also been updated to cover setting up inbound webhooks. I think what you described would be a fairly elegant solution to the problem. I didn't test it (someone probably should :smile:) but I believe `has_pending_deployment` is correct. `latest_common_version` does look at `archive` not `live`. The function that looks at `live` is `current_version` which is also used in `has_pending_deployment`.

In pseudocode, my understanding of `has_pending_deployment` is:
```
smallest_current := version pointed at by symlinks
largest_archive := latest version in archive
return smallest_current < largest_archive
```
This seems correct. I believe `smallest_current` is effectively the version pointed at by symlinks. More accurately, the pseudocode would be something like `smallest version pointed to by any symlink` but because of the use of `_fix_symlinks`, I believe they should all point to the same version. I think the code is fine as it is though as it's trying to be defensive.

I agree this needs to be cleared up in the documentation, perhaps in the install script.

If what you need is to get your own copy of this repo (pydatalab) into your locally-built datalab image then it's easier to do it while building the datalab image. To do this you can pass the path to your local pydatalab to the build script at containers/datalab/build.sh, for example:
`./build.sh ../../pydatalab/` < note the slash in the end

SOCIAL_AUTH_SPOTIFY_EXTRA_DATA = [

]
```

I'm just going to weigh in here with relation to the RTMPDump dev not working on it any more. Why should they? It's the same reason Chrippa stopped working on livestreamer, at some point there is no incentive to do so. No one pays you to work on these projects, you get tired of answering questions and dealing with issues. It doesn't provide you any financial security to dedicate time to it even though there are 10s of 1000s of downloads a month, and you could spend that time more productively elsewhere, so why continue?

Who cares if you let some random person down, or that the software has vulnerabilities? You're not being reimbursed to work on it and you're spending your limited time, so there's no reason to do so. It happens to every developer at some point unless they get hired by an organization to work on the software, and even then they'll likely leave at some point.

So before we start talking about conspiracies, let's just approach it as sensible humans, you want to be rewarded for your work in a way that enhances your situation, 99% of open source projects do not receive this support, and thus people stop contributing, it's as simple as that.

`interact` returns a function, and the tab expects widgets as children, so that explains the error in trying to use the return value of `interact`. `interactive` works better since it returns a widget, but the initial_condition function just *returns* the widget, it doesn't display it, so you won't see the init condition controls there. Furthermore, it looks like every time the checkbox is toggled, you're creating new controls for the initial conditions, so it will be hard to keep a reference to them to do something with the data.

If I were you, I'd create the controls for the init conditions and put them in a box. Then create a checkbox, and use that checkbox to show/hide that box. Or I'd use an Accordion, which has that hide/show logic builtin:

```python
import ipywidgets as widgets

def initial_condition(): 
    label_iniCond = widgets.HTML("""The Initial elevation file dictates the 
         dry/wet zones of the bathymetry. The initial U and V velocity files specifies 
        the velocities in the x and y directions. They must be uploaded in 
        the Jupyter Notebook Home folder before running the simulation.""",
        layout = widgets.Layout(height = '45px', width = '90%',
                    size = '20'))

    space_box = widgets.Box(layout=widgets.Layout(height ='25px', width='90%')) 
    # this ^ box is created to have space among widgets 

    # initial elevation widget container (label and textbox)
    label_iniElev = widgets.Label('Initial Elevation Text File:',
    layout =  widgets.Layout(width = '18%'))
    iniElev_text = widgets.Text(layout=widgets.Layout(width = "50%",height = '85px'))
    container_iniElev = widgets.HBox(children=[label_iniElev,iniElev_text])

    # initial u vel widget container (label and textbox)
    label_Uvel = widgets.Label('Initial U Velocity Text File:', 
    layout = widgets.Layout(width = '18%'))
    Uvel_text = widgets.Text(layout = iniElev_text.layout)
    container_Uvel = widgets.HBox(children=[label_Uvel,Uvel_text])

    # initial v vel widget container (label and textbox)
    label_Vvel = widgets.Label('Initial V Velocity Text File:', 
    layout = widgets.Layout(width = '18%'))
    Vvel_text = widgets.Text(layout = iniElev_text.layout)
    container_Vvel = widgets.HBox(children=[label_Vvel,Vvel_text])

    iniCond_box = widgets.VBox(children=[label_iniCond,space_box,container_iniElev,
                                container_Uvel,container_Vvel])
    return iniCond_box

initial = initial_condition()

accordion = widgets.Accordion([initial])
accordion.set_title(0, 'Initial Conditions')
accordion
```

@CFAndy I pushed a little bit further the tests I made. The `notation.runtime` project is tricky, it modify some properties at runtime, but I succeeded to deserialize, modify and serialize again a test project I found on github. However, there is some modifications I had to perform by hand that are consequences of:

1. a current restriction of `pyecore/pyecoregen` (nothing too much painful to do, only add one line), 
1. a missing information in the serialized model by PyEcore when dealing with `href`,
1. a missing option for XMI serialization/deserialization,
1. manual code modifications that exist in `runtime.notation`.

For the point 1, the modification is pretty easy. This case is currently a kind of restriction of `pyecore/pyecoregen`, I will try to correct it someday, but at the moment, I don't know how to properly detect this case.

For the point 2 and 3, I will patch PyEcore and provide very quickly a new version, perhaps this week-end if I have some free time. Point 2 is already fixed in my local branch, and point 3 should be easy to deal with.

For the point 4 however, there is no other solution than manually add some code to deal with the 'extravaganza' of the `runtime.notation` project. For example, some properties are dynamically renamed. This information is buried in the code, and is not expressed in the metamodel: https://bugs.eclipse.org/bugs/show_bug.cgi?id=159226#c12. This is possible to deal with this PyEcore, but there is no automatic solution for this.

Here is the code I have at the moment. I use the `.ecorediag` which uses gmf-notation, and the `.ecore` from this address (this ecorediag gives the graphical representation of an ecore metamodel):
ecorediag -> https://gist.github.com/whirlwin/5347021 (named: footballmanagement.ecorediag)
ecore -> https://gist.github.com/whirlwin/5347275 (named: footballmanagement.ecore)

Please note that this code only works with the patches I currently have on my local branch

```Python
import notation
from pyecore.resources import ResourceSet


# Dynamically add the new property, otherwise the '.ecorediag'
# see https://bugs.eclipse.org/bugs/show_bug.cgi?id=159226#c12
notation.View.persistedChildren.name = 'children'
notation.View.children = notation.View.persistedChildren
notation.Diagram.persistedEdges.name = 'edges'
notation.Diagram.edges = notation.Diagram.persistedEdges


# This class is here to match the 'runtime.notation' project (not expressed in the metamodel)
# see: https://github.com/eclipse/gmf.notation/blob/master/org.eclipse.gmf.runtime.notation/src/org/eclipse/gmf/runtime/notation/datatype/RelativeBendpoint.java
# This class and functions define dedicated deserialization/serialization for the RelativeBendpointList
# so it match the 'runtime.notation' behavior
class BendPoint(object):
    def __init__(self, source_x, source_y, target_x, target_y):
        self.source_x = source_x
        self.source_y = source_y
        self.target_x = target_x
        self.target_y = target_y

    def __repr__(self):
        return '[{}, {}, {}, {}]'.format(self.source_x, self.source_y,
                                         self.target_x, self.target_y)


def from_str(s):
    result = []
    for line in s.split('$'):
        v = [int(i) for i in line[1:-1].split(',')]
        result.append(BendPoint(v[0], v[1], v[2], v[3]))
    return result


def to_str(o):
    s = '{o!r}'.format(o=o)
    return s.replace('], [', ']$[')[1:-1]


notation.RelativeBendpointList.from_string = from_str
notation.RelativeBendpointList.to_string = to_str


# From this point, this is basic PyEcore/EMF handling
# we create a resourceset and register the 'notation' metamodel
rset = ResourceSet()
rset.metamodel_registry[notation.nsURI] = notation

# we get the '.ecore' (not required if you don't follow the cross-references from the ecorediag)
# resource = rset.get_resource('footballmanagement.ecore')
# ecore_root = resource.contents[0]

# we get the ecorediag
resource = rset.get_resource('footballmanagement.ecorediag')
root = resource.contents[0]

# we get one 'Edge'
e = [x for x in root.eAllContents() if isinstance(x, notation.Edge)][0]
e.bendpoints.points.append(BendPoint(0, 0, 0, 0))

# just for test, we save the resource in another '.xmi'
resource.save(output='other.xmi')
```

the naming convention will be:

All moban extensions will have `moban` as prefix, i.e. `moban-mako`, `moban-haml` ..

All moban templates(so called mobans) packages, will have `mobans` as suffix, i.e. `pypi-mobans`, `pyexcel-mobans`. 

And there will be an exercise to convert all mobans repo as python packages. These converted packages will be acting as equivalent of cookie cutter packages. However, the difference will be: moban template packages are libraries, whereas cookie cutter packages are "executable" because cookie cutter will make scaffolding out from a single package. In order to cover this gap, it will make sense to include yehua related files with mobans so that, yehua could command yehua files and moban will take mobans as template libraries.

Hi Peter,

at the moment `data_source` argument has to match the layer's data source configured in [Configurator](https://apps.sentinel-hub.com/configurator/). This is because for a given layer Sentinel Hub services can only provide data from the layer configuration's data source. E.g. if the layer is configured to "Sentinel S2 - L1C" you cannot get Sentinel-2 L2A data from it even if you specify that with `data_source` parameter in your request.

If you set the `data_source` parameter incorrectly you can get a few different types of errors (e.g. the one you described above). None of them gives the correct explanation of the problem. Therefore we will try to make some improvements about this in the future.
